title: How Psychedelic Therapy Addresses Codependency and People-Pleasing
body:
How Psychedelic Therapy Addresses Codependency and People-Pleasing

1. Opening Hook: When Saying Yes Becomes Saying No

Years ago, I found myself trapped in a cycle of saying “yes” to everything and everyone around me, even when every fiber of my being screamed for a break. I was the classic people-pleaser, the one who wore happiness for others like a uniform and buried my own needs under a pile of obligations. At first, I thought it was just kindness, a way to show love. But over time, I realized I was losing my sense of self, drowning in codependency, and the people I thought I was helping started to take more than I could give. It was exhausting and soul-crushing.

It took a psychedelic experience to crack that shell wide open. During a guided psilocybin session, the walls I had built around my own feelings fell away. I saw the roots of my people-pleasing patterns - pain, fear, and old wounds that had nothing to do with the present moment. Suddenly, I wasn’t just reacting to others’ needs; I was witnessing the dance of my own inner life. That moment shifted everything.

If you’re reading this, maybe you’re like me. Maybe you’ve spent years bending toward others, losing your voice, your boundaries, your power. Codependency and people-pleasing aren’t just annoying habits. They’re survival strategies, often born in childhood or past trauma, that keep us stuck in cycles of pain and exhaustion. Psychedelic therapy offers a rare chance to see these patterns clearly, to feel them deeply, and to begin changing from the inside out.

In this article, I want to walk you through how psychedelic therapy can open doors for people caught in these patterns. We’ll explore the science, the stories, and the practical steps that can help you reclaim your life from codependency’s grip. If you’re tired of always putting others first at the cost of your own wellbeing, this might be the beginning of something different.

2. Understanding Codependency and People-Pleasing: A Psychedelic Lens

At its core, codependency is a tangled web of emotional entanglement where your sense of self becomes blurred with others’ approval, needs, or emotions. People-pleasing is one face of this coin, a way to seek validation externally, often to avoid feelings of rejection or abandonment. From a psychological standpoint, these patterns often arise from early attachment wounds or trauma, where love and safety felt conditional.

Neuroscience sheds light on how these patterns get wired into our brains. In codependent individuals, the amygdala - the brain’s alarm system - can become hyperactive, constantly scanning for threats to relationship security. This keeps us on edge, eager to respond to others’ needs to prevent perceived abandonment. At the same time, areas like the prefrontal cortex, which help regulate emotions and maintain boundaries, can become underactive or overwhelmed.

Enter psychedelics, substances like psilocybin or LSD, which temporarily disrupt typical brain activity and connectivity. Research by Robin Carhart-Harris and colleagues at Imperial College shows how psychedelics reduce the activity of the brain’s default mode network (DMN), a hub linked to rigid self-concepts and habitual thought patterns. This quieting of the DMN allows for new perspectives to arise, breaking free from old narrative loops.

For people-pleasers, this means the constant internal script of “I must be perfect, I must keep others happy” can soften. The heightened emotional sensitivity that drives codependency can become a source of insight rather than reactivity. In this altered state, individuals often report a strengthened connection to their authentic self, a rediscovery of boundaries that were lost under the weight of obligation.

Stanislav Grof, one of the pioneers of psychedelic therapy, described this as accessing “the inner healer.” Psychedelics help people see the origins of their suffering and release the grip of old trauma - especially those wounds tied to attachment and self-worth. It’s not a quick fix, but the medicine can open the door to a new way of relating to oneself and others.

3. Research and Evidence: What the Science Says About Psychedelic Therapy for Codependency

While psychedelic research is still catching up to the mental health realities many have faced for decades, there’s growing evidence supporting its role in untangling patterns like codependency and people-pleasing. Johns Hopkins University, where Roland Griffiths leads seminal studies, has shown psilocybin’s ability to reduce anxiety and depression by reshaping how the brain processes self-critical thoughts. This is relevant because people-pleasers often struggle with harsh self-judgment when they don’t meet their own impossible standards.

A study published in the Journal of Psychopharmacology in 2018 examined how psilocybin-assisted therapy affected patients with treatment-resistant depression and anxiety. Many participants described a newfound sense of self-acceptance and a decrease in obsessive behaviors, which are frequently connected to codependent tendencies.

Another important study by Carhart-Harris et al. in 2017 demonstrated that psychedelic sessions create “neural plasticity,” an increased ability for the brain to form new connections and break free from entrenched patterns. This plasticity is crucial for people stuck in codependency because it allows them to re-wire their responses to interpersonal triggers and build healthier relationship dynamics.

MDMA, while not a classic psychedelic, has shown promise in treating post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD), which often underpins codependent behavior. The phase 3 trials led by Michael Mithoefer revealed that MDMA-assisted therapy helps patients approach painful memories without being overwhelmed. This safe confrontation with trauma can loosen the emotional chains that bind codependent individuals to their roles and behaviors.

Adding to this picture, Rick Doblin and the Multidisciplinary Association for Psychedelic Studies (MAPS) have been exploring how psychedelic therapy enhances emotional regulation and empathy, paradoxically allowing people to become less reactive yet more connected - to themselves and others. This balance is essential to breaking free from people-pleasing, which often comes from either emotional numbness or flooding.

If you want to understand the science behind these breakthroughs, Michael Pollan’s How to Change Your Mind is an excellent resource that combines research with human stories. It’s a reminder that we’re only beginning to grasp how these medicines can heal wounds that conventional therapy sometimes leaves untouched.

4. Practical Application: Using Psychedelic Therapy to Shift Codependent Patterns

So how does this all come together for someone who struggles with codependency and people-pleasing? First, it’s vital to recognize that psychedelic therapy is not about flipping a switch overnight. It’s a process that requires preparation, intention, and careful guidance. Working with an experienced facilitator or therapist is key to creating a safe space where vulnerable feelings can emerge and be explored.

Before a session, I recommend journaling to clarify your goals and fears. Writing can help you bring unconscious patterns to light. If you’re looking for a good journaling companion, something like the Journaling Notebook Leather Bound can make this practice feel special and grounded. Writing before and after sessions lets you track your insights and emotional shifts.

During the psychedelic experience, you might notice a heightened awareness of your usual responses. For example, a phrase like “I need to make everyone happy” may come into focus as a defense mechanism rather than a truth. Psychedelic therapy can help you access emotions you’ve buried, such as fear of rejection or anger at having your needs ignored. Feeling these emotions fully - without judgment - allows them to lose their power to control you.

After the session, integration work is essential. This means discussing your experience with your therapist, continuing journaling, and practicing setting boundaries in small, real-life interactions. You might begin by saying “no” in safe situations and observing how it feels. Building this muscle takes time but becomes easier when you’ve touched the deeper cause of your codependency in your sessions.

Another tool I’ve found invaluable is learning to listen to the body. Bessel van der Kolk’s The Body Keeps the Score explains how trauma lives in bodily sensations, and psychedelics often amplify this awareness. Paying attention to physical cues can alert you to when you’re slipping into old patterns, like a tightening in the chest or a hollow feeling in the stomach when you’re about to people-please.

Lastly, community matters. Sharing your journey with others who understand these struggles can reinforce your growth. Whether through group integration circles or trusted friends, connection helps replace the isolation codependency feeds on.

5. Common Challenges and How to Work Through Them

Psychedelic therapy is powerful, but it isn’t without challenges. For people with codependency, certain obstacles often arise that can feel daunting. One major challenge is the temptation to use the psychedelic experience as a quick fix or escape. Since people-pleasers tend to avoid discomfort, they might hope the medicine will erase their pain without effort. I’ve seen that expectation lead to disappointment.

Another hurdle is confronting the deep-seated shame that underlies codependent behavior. Psychedelic experiences often bring this shame to the surface in vivid ways. It can feel unbearable and trigger intense emotional reactions. That’s why working with a skilled guide who can help you stay grounded and compassionate is essential. Developing practices like focusing on breath, mindfulness, or gentle movement can help soothe these moments.

Integration difficulties also crop up. You might have a breakthrough during a session but struggle to translate it into daily life changes. The pull of old habits is strong. When faced with conflict or criticism, the urge to people-please can resurface fast. Addressing this requires patience and perseverance. Creating a structured integration plan - regular journaling, therapy check-ins, and boundary-setting exercises - will help keep the momentum.

Physical and mental health conditions can complicate psychedelic therapy, too. If you have a history of psychosis or certain cardiac issues, psychedelics can be risky. It’s important to screen carefully and choose appropriate medicines and doses. Codependency often goes hand-in-hand with anxiety or depression, so having a solid mental health foundation before psychedelic work is wise.

Finally, there’s the fear of losing loved ones or being rejected once you stop people-pleasing. This fear can paralyze change. Talking openly about these fears with your therapist and trusted supports can shift the perspective. You don’t have to face it alone, and the people who truly care want you whole - not just your version of yourself as a pleaser.

6. Integration with Daily Life: A Long-Term Perspective

One psychedelic session will not cure codependency overnight. Healing is a marathon, not a sprint. The real magic lies in how you carry your insights forward, day after day, into your relationships and self-care. Integration is the bridge between the altered state and your everyday life.

After psychedelic therapy, I encourage people to start small with setting boundaries. This could mean declining a request that feels draining or speaking up about your feelings honestly. At first, this might evoke guilt or anxiety - that old script telling you you’re selfish. But each time you honor your truth, you reinforce a new internal message: “I matter.”

Mindfulness practices help maintain this awareness. When you notice the impulse to people-please, pause and check in with your body and mind. Are you acting out of fear, habit, or genuine compassion? Are you protecting your energy or sacrificing it? These moments become opportunities for choice instead of autopilot.

Building a support network is also crucial. Find friends or groups who respect your boundaries and encourage your growth. Community helps counteract the isolation that codependency often breeds.

Reading and continued education deepen understanding. After my psychedelic experiences, I found books like The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk invaluable for connecting trauma’s physical imprints to emotional patterns. This kind of learning keeps me curious and compassionate with myself through the ups and downs.

Returning to journaling keeps the process visible. Writing down your struggles, victories, and reflections can reveal patterns and progress that may not be obvious day-to-day. Using a dedicated journal, such as the Journaling Notebook Leather Bound, makes this feel like a sacred ritual rather than a chore.

Eventually, the goal isn’t to be perfect but to be present and authentic. Psychedelic therapy plants the seed, but the soil is the life you build afterward - the choices you make to honor yourself and your relationships with real honesty.

7. Closing Reflection: The Unseen Freedom in Saying No

In my experience, the hardest and most beautiful lesson psychedelic therapy taught me was the power of saying no. Not as a weapon or a wall, but as an open door to freedom. Saying no to others’ expectations isn’t a betrayal - it’s a gift to yourself and everyone around you. It reshapes the relationship from codependent to connected, from fear-driven to love-driven.

People-pleasing and codependency often masquerade as care, but beneath the surface, they are cries for validation and safety. Psychedelic therapy helps illuminate these hidden messages and gives you a chance to answer them differently. You realize that your worth isn’t tied to how much you give, but to simply being who you are.

This shift isn’t just about you; it ripples outward. When you stop sacrificing your own needs, your relationships can breathe and grow more authentic. The people you love get to know the real you, not the version filtered through anxiety and obligation.

There’s a freedom in this that’s hard to describe - like stepping into sunlight after years in shadow. It doesn’t mean the path is easy or straight. Boundaries will be tested, old habits will call you back, and the work continues. But with each honest no, you claim a piece of yourself back, and in doing so, you invite the possibility of a life lived on your own terms.

For anyone wrestling with codependency and people-pleasing, psychedelic therapy is not a magic bullet, but it is a powerful lantern on the path. It shines light on the unseen chains and offers a glimpse beyond them. And that glimpse can be enough to keep stepping forward.
